Summary equation for photosynthesis:
(this should look familiar)

This was cellular respiration:

thylakoids

Calvin Cycle – 3 phases
Convert inorganic carbon (CO 2 ) to an organic molecule, 3-phosphoglycerate
Convert (reduce) 3-phosphoglycerate to glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate
Recreate the starting molecules so the cycle can be repeated with more CO 2
The Calvin cycle

Carbon Fixation
RuBP = Ribulose-BisPhosphate 5 Carbons, 2 Phosphates
RuBP +  CO 2   unstable 6 Carbon, 2 Phosphate intermediate
2 molecules of 3-phosphoglycerate
( each 3 Carbons, 1 Phosphate )
spontaneous breakdown
Convert inorganic carbon (CO 2 ) to an organic molecule, 3-phosphoglycerate

RuBP +  CO 2 2 x 3-phosphoglycerate
rubisco
rubisco = Ribulose-bisphosphate carboxylase/ oygenase
the most abundant enzyme on the planet
20% - 50% of all protein in chloroplasts
SLOW  -  3 reactions/second
sloppy – will take O 2 instead of CO 2 as substrate

RuBP +  CO 2 unstable 6 Carbon, 2 Phosphate intermediate
2 molecules of 3-phosphoglycerate
( each 3 Carbons, 1 Phosphate )
spontaneous breakdown
rubisco
3-phosphoglycerate ≠ glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate
need glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to make glucose
need glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to regenerate RuBP

takes lots of energy
u ses up ATP and
oxidizes NADPH
from light reactions
moves energy from
high-energy, unstable
molecules to stable
storage molecules
Convert (reduce) 3-phosphoglycerate to glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate

RuBP +  CO 2 2 x 3-phosphoglycerate
rubisco
3-phosphoglycerate                          glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate
each 3-phosphoglycerate uses 1 ATP and oxidizes 1 NADPH  NADP +
need to “fix” 6 Carbons (capture 6 CO 2 ) to make 1 glucose (C 6 H 12 O 6 )

convert 5 molecules of G3P
3 carbons, 1 phosphate
to
3 molecules RuBP
5 carbons, 2 phosphates
usually written for 3 CO 2 – but takes 6 CO 2 to make one glucose

Calvin cycle summary
Inputs
6 CO 2
18 ATP
12 NADPH
Outputs
1 Glucose = C 6 H 12 O 6
18 ADP  +  18 P i
12 NADPH +
Storing energy from ATP, high-energy electrons in NADPH in
carbon-carbon bonds in carbohydrate (glucose)
It takes/costs 18 ATP to make one glucose –
is this a good investment?

photosynthesis

Photorespiration
rubsico is sloppy – will take O 2 instead of CO 2 if [O 2 ] > [CO 2 ]

Photorespiration
2-phosphoglycolate ( don’t learn name! ) – not an energy molecule
does not produce glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate
2 Carbons are lost (breaks down to CO 2 )
uses up ATP to break down
doesn’t regenerate all RuBP
3 C, 1 P
2 C, 2 P

gases move by diffusion:
CO 2 enters leaf through stoma ( stomate ; plural = stomata )
O 2 (waste product) and H 2 O leave

Photorespiration
occurs when O 2 is more available as substrate than CO 2
hot, dry environments – keep stomata closed to reduce H 2 O loss
O 2 produced by light reactions
[ O 2 ]  >  [ CO 2 ]
CO 2 used up by Calvin cycle

C 4 photosynthesis
use PEP Carboxylase, not rubisco , for carbon capture
fix CO 2 into 4-carbon molecule, not 3-phosphoglycerate
transfer to cells deeper in leaf = less O 2
not doing light reactions
used by grasses, prairie plants

CAM photosynthesis
Crassulacean Acid Metabolism
fix CO 2 at night – cooler, less water loss when stomata open
use PEP Carboxylase, not rubisco , for carbon capture
fix CO 2 into 4-carbon molecule, store as malic acid in central vacuole
release CO 2 during day for Calvin cycle
malic
acid

CAM photosynthesis
Crassulacean Acid Metabolism
used by desert plants: cactus, succulents , agave
also pineapple, some bromeliads
Kalanchoe – the CAM plant you’ll use
same cell
